grammarChart.title: Passive Voice (be + past participle)
grammarChart.structure: Subject + be (am/is/are/was/were/been/being) + Past Participle (V3) (+ by + agent)

tenses.active: They are going to build houses.
tenses.passive: Houses are going to be built (by them).

explanation: The passive voice is used when the action is more important than the doer of the action, or when the doer is unknown or obvious.  In the passive voice, the object of the active sentence becomes the subject of the passive sentence. The structure involves the verb 'be' in the appropriate tense, followed by the past participle of the main verb. The original subject (the agent) can be omitted or introduced with 'by'.
keyRules: Identify the object in the active sentence; it becomes the subject in the passive sentence.
Use the correct form of the auxiliary verb 'be' (am, is, are, was, were, been, being) according to the original tense and the new subject.
Use the past participle (V3) of the main verb.
The agent (the doer of the action) is optional and can be introduced with 'by'.
Passive voice is common in formal writing and academic texts.
azarNotes: Think of it like changing the focus. In the active voice, we focus on WHO did it. In the passive voice, we focus on WHAT was done. For IELTS, using passive voice appropriately shows your ability to vary sentence structure and write formally.

examples.active: The company launched a new product last year.
examples.passive: A new product was launched by the company last year.

examples.active: Scientists are conducting research into climate change.
examples.passive: Research into climate change is being conducted by scientists.

examples.active: The government has implemented new policies.
examples.passive: New policies have been implemented by the government.

examples.active: Students must submit their assignments by Friday.
examples.passive: Assignments must be submitted by students by Friday.

commonMistakes: Using the wrong form of 'be'. For example, 'Houses is built' instead of 'Houses are built'.
Forgetting to use the past participle. For example, 'Houses were build' instead of 'Houses were built'.
Overusing the passive voice, making the writing sound unnatural or vague.
Incorrectly forming the passive of modal verbs. For example, 'Houses can build' instead of 'Houses can be built'.
Confusing the active and passive structures, especially with verbs that can be both intransitive and transitive.
ieltsTip: In IELTS Writing Task 2, the passive voice is very useful for introducing a topic or stating a general fact without needing to mention the specific agent. For example, 'It is widely believed that...' or 'Many people are concerned about...'. It also helps to maintain objectivity in academic writing.

quiz.question: Choose the correct passive form: The report ______ by the committee yesterday.
Change the active sentence to passive: The chef is preparing a special meal.

options.a: A special meal is preparing by the chef.
options.b: A special meal is being prepared by the chef.
options.c: A special meal was being prepared by the chef.
options.d: A special meal will be prepared by the chef.

quiz.question: Which sentence uses the passive voice correctly?

options.a: The book has been read by millions.
options.b: The book has read by millions.
options.c: The book is read by millions.
options.d: The book had reading by millions.

quiz.question: Complete the sentence with the correct passive form:  All applications ______ by the end of the month.
Change the active sentence to passive:  They will announce the results next week.

options.a: The results will announce next week.
options.b: The results will be announced next week.
options.c: The results are announced next week.
options.d: The results will have announced next week.
